entry.title: 🇳🇱 SAP Cloud Application Programming Model with SAP HANA Cloud
entry.content:
This SAP CodeJam event is about combining the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model with SAP HANA Cloud to create full-stack applications. In this CodeJam we will learn how to deploy and configure an instance of the SAP HANA Cloud trial, develop a multi-target application using SAP Business Application Studio and the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model, and create a service layer and SAP Fiori UI that also includes SAP HANA native artifacts such as calculation views.

This is an in-person event only (not virtual) and is planned for Thu 26 Sep 2024 in Eindhoven, NL. The language of the event and the content will be English.

The event is being kindly hosted by McCoy & Partners at the Kennispoort business centre, thank you! The specific location of the event is their offices in that business centre (see the Eindhoven address in their contact page, and the event location detail map here).

Please aim to arrive at 0900 local time to get set up and plugged in, for a start at 0930 local time. We should be finished by around 1600 local time.

The instructor is Developer Advocate DJ Adams.

There are essential prerequisites that must be completed by every attendee before the event. They are documented here: https://github.com/SAP-samples/cap-hana-exercises-codejam/blob/main/prerequisites.md.  

Please note that spaces are limited. Register here on this Community Event page using the RSVP functionality.

For more information on SAP CodeJam events, to ask questions, and to chat with your fellow attendees, head over to the SAP CodeJam group.

entry.title: Create an Issue in SAP Quality Issue Resolution from an Alert in SAP Digital Manufacturing
entry.content:
The Alert Service in SAP Digital Manufacturing introduces human decision making into the resolution of exceptions happening on the shopfloor. You can now leverage a new powerful integration scenario that lets you react to alerts by creating an issue in SAP Quality Issue Resolution directly from the Manage Alerts app.

Introduction

The Alert Service in SAP Digital Manufacturing aims to notify production operators or supervisors about events or exceptions happening on the shop floor and introduce human decision making when responding to them. Alerts can either be created manually within PODs or automatically by production processes. They contain, as alert properties, the relevant manufacturing execution context such as plant, work center, resource, operation, material, SFC, order, and so on.

 

All alerts appear in the “Manage Alerts” app on the Fiori launchpad of SAP Digital Manufacturing and are managed there. Managing alerts means analyzing and acting upon the alerts and promoting them within their (customizable) status sequence: New >> Acknowledged >> In Process >> Completed.

Certainly, alerts can be acted upon outside the SAP Digital Manufacturing application so that only status changes of alerts are maintained by users processing alerts in the Manage Alerts app until they finally reach the status “Completed”.

However, the Alert Service lets you configure action buttons for the different alert types. Action buttons enable alert processors to directly trigger - from the Manage Alerts app - production processes specifically designed to respond to alerts or send out predefined notification emails to users who can contribute to resolving the alerts.

SAP Digital Manufacturing comes with different alert types predefined by SAP – among them the alert type “Quality Request”, which customers can use when creating alerts to respond to quality events, for example, the discovery of quality issues during the production process. Alternatively, customers can define their own alert types.

Customers who use both solutions, SAP Digital Manufacturing and SAP Quality Issue Resolution (QIR), can leverage a new API made available in QIR to create “issues” in QIR from action buttons in alerts, for example, configured for alerts of type “Quality Request” (and similarly for custom alert types – in our example “Quality Alert”).

Alert properties providing manufacturing execution context information and additional free text entries can be passed on as payload to the API when clicking on the action button. As a result, a new “issue” is created in QIR where it is picked up by a quality engineer who decides whether it needs to be turned into a more formal problem-solving process to resolve the issue in a collaborative and fully documented manner alongside a standard methodology such as “8 Disciplines (8D)” adopted by the discrete industries the customers is part of.

Flow at Runtime

Let’s start by walking through the flow from an end user perspective. The persona who creates the alert on the shop floor is usually a production operator, worker, or a supervisor. If you are using the SAP predefined alert type “Quality Request”, the user triggers the alert from one of your PODs (Production Operator Dashboards) via the “Raise Alerts” POD plugin as shown below. It allows the user  to enter an alert description manually, define an alert processor and recipients for in-app notifications, and choose if a notification email is to be sent as well.

The other option is to create custom alert types. Custom alert types cannot be created from the “Raise Alert” plugin. Instead, customers need to customize their PODs and add an additional button to their PODs and bind a production process to the button that creates the alert. In the screenshot below, the yellow button “Quality Alert” has been added to the POD. When you click this button, an alert of custom type “Quality Alert” is created. Here, the content and recipients of email and in-app notifications sent upon alert creation are configured at design time within the alert type. However, it provides customers with a degree of freedom to define alert properties that best match the purpose. Alert properties can contain manufacturing execution context information or values of sensor tags.

Besides manual creation of alerts, it is also possible to create alerts of either type automatically while running production processes. Alerts can be accessed directly from the email and in-app notifications or via the “Manage Alerts” app on the Fiori launchpad. The recipients in a quality context are usually quality engineers, dispatchers, or supervisors.

New in-app notifications are highlighted with a red circle that appears right next to the bell icon on the top right of the launchpad.

Clicking the bell icon opens the notification bar. Clicking on the message opens the details view of the alert that generated the notification.

Notification emails contain a link at the bottom that also opens the alert details view.

The alert details view contains alert header information, the alert property values providing context information, and buttons to change the alert processor or to change the alert status.In addition, they can contain action buttons, if defined for their respective alert type. In the example below, two action buttons are available. The action button “Notify Quality Engineers” sends a predefined email to a predefined list of recipients. When you click this action button, you have the option to change both the email and the list of recipients.

The other action button “Create Issue” creates an issue calling an API in QIR. A popup showing the required input parameter appears (see below). The first one “plant” is prefilled from the alert property “DME_Plant” whose value was taken from the manufacturing execution context from which the alert was created in the first place. The second parameter “descriptionShort” is a field required in QIR to create an alert. The alert processor can enter free text before clicking OK. He or she can now change the status of the alert to “Completed”.

This is where the flow ends in SAP Digital Manufacturing.

Next in the flow is a quality engineer logging on to SAP Quality Issue Resolution (QIR) to check for new issues in the “Manage Issues” app:

He or she can use filters to search for issues. The issue listed below is the one created from the alert:

You can see the new issue with the name “paint peels off from bike frame”. This name, the plant ID “1010”, and plant name “Plant Walldorf” were taken from the payload of the API call. All other fields are left blank. The quality engineer can edit the issue and add missing information and decide whether to create a problem-solving process for the issue or rather to end the processing of the issue after an initial analysis and to set it to complete. In this example, we have provided only the minimum set of required payload parameters to create the issue in QIR via API call from SAP Digital Manufacturing. In the next sections you will learn more about different ways to enrich the payload to provide additional context for the issue.

 

Prerequisites

You require administrator access to the following apps in SAP Digital Manufacturing:

  • Manage Web Servers
  • Manage Service Registry
  • Design Production Processes
  • Manage Alert Types (role Alerts_Admin)

You require a subscription to SAP Quality Issue Resolution and on its launchpad you need access to this app:

  • Manage Issues

You need to ask your tenant administrator to create the following destination to your QIR tenant in BTP Cockpit for your SAP Digital Manufacturing subaccount:

Name: QIR_QIssue_Destination
Type: HTTP
URL:<URL of issues api endpoint from service key JSON>
Proxy Type: Internet
Authentication: OAuth2ClientCredentials
Client ID: <your Client ID from service key JSON>
Client Secret: <your Client Secret from service key JSON>
Token Service URL Type: Dedicated
Token Service URL: <your URL from in service key JSON>/oauth/token

You can look up the needed values in the service key JSON of your QIR service instance.

 

For more information on how to create a service instance and service key, see the Administration Guide for SAP Quality Issue Resolution.

Overview of Configuration Steps

The configuration for the full scenario can be divided into two parts:

  • Alert generation
  • Alert response via action button

The alert generation part is not covered in this blog. It is covered already in the following documentation:

  • Configuration Guide “Enable DM Alert Service 2405” attached to SAP Note 3435403
  • An updated version of this configuration guide will be published as of release 2408 as a new section in the Operations Guide for SAP Digital Manufacturing
  • Documentation of the DM Alert Service in the SAP Help Portal
  • Application Help for POD Designer explaining how to customize PODs, add buttons and bind production processes to them

This blog focuses on the configuration of the alert response via action button, in our example, creating issues in QIR via action button in alerts.

Since action buttons can be configured either to send predefined emails or trigger production processes, we first need to configure a production process that creates an issue in QIR via API call. For this, we need to create a respective service that does that. Hence, the sequence of configuration steps is as follows:

  1. Create a web server requiring the HTTP destination mentioned in the prerequisites section.
  2. Create a request body and response schemas for the service
  3. Create the service calling the API to create issues in QIR
  4. Create a production process feeding required payload parameters for the API call
  5. Configure an action button for our example custom alert type “Quality Alert”

Create Web Server

In SAP Digital Manufacturing, you need to create a web server that reflects the QIR application.

  1. Open the “Manage Web Servers” app on the Fiori Launchpad and click Create
  2. We have given it the name QIR_QIssue_Webserver; don’t enter a plant
  3. Under Service Details, choose type “Cloud services”
  4. Enter as host URL your URL from the service key JSON as explained in above section
  5. Then, add the destination mentioned in the prerequisites section and save.
  6. Now open the web server “DMC_Cloud”.
  7. In the “Connection” section, click Create and search for the web server “QIR_QIssue_Webserver” you have just created. Select it and then save it.

Create Schemas in Service Registry

You need to create four schemas in the “Manage Service Registry” app. The schemas are attached to this blog as txt file with the respective name. Click on the “Schemas” tab and proceed as follows for each one of the four schemas listed below: Click on the Create button, then click on “Switch to Code” and copy the schema from the respective txt file  into the schema definition frame of the UI and save.

  1. Request body schema QIR_Issue_RequestBody
  2. Subschema to_TextsSchema.
  3. Subschema to_ProductsSchema.
  4. Response schema QIR_Issue_Response.

Now, include the second schema to_TextsSchema as StructureArray to the third schema to_ProductsSchema by opening the latter schema in Edit mode, clicking on “Add”, and placing the cursor in the last highlighted line. Enter “to_Texts” as the name, select “StructureArray” as data type, and select to_TextsSchema from the library as schema. Choose Save.

Finally, add schema to_ProductsSchema as structure to the first schema QIR_Issue_RequestBody. Make sure you select “Structure” as data type this time:

Create Service in Service Registry

Create a new service in the “Manage Service Registry” app, which calls the API endpoint in QIR to create issues and handles the request body and response payloads. Make sure you select the web server created in the section above and enter as endpoint /odata/v4/IssueService/Issues .
Use the same values for Type, Method, Protocol as shown in the screenshot below.

 In the request body section choose “application/json” as content type with data type “Structure” and select the QIR_Issue_RequestBody schema from the library.

 In the response section, add the line as shown in the screenshot below and select the schema “QIR_Issue_Response” from the library.

Design Production Process to Call the Service

The next step is to create a production process, which calls the service you have just created:

  1.  Open the “Design Production Process” app on the launchpad and create a new production process design:

     



  2. Click on the Create button on the canvas. Enter a name for the new production process and select “Cloud” as runtime type.
  3. Click on the three dots “…” highlighted in the screenshot below and activate the toggle “Publish to Service Registry”:

  4. Click on the pencil icon and on “Select Services” in the left pane, and check boxes along the path within the web server “QIR_QIssue_Webserver”. Then click on Save.
  5. You can now draw the production process in the graphical editor of the designer tool. Drag and drop the Start control, the QIR_Issue_Creation service, and the End control from the left pane into the production process canvas. Then connect the Start control with the rectangular service box and the box with the Endcontrol by selecting the start object, clicking on the arrow icon that is shown on the top right and drawing it to the destination object, i.e. the service box and the End control, respectively.

     

Once you’re done, the process should appear as shown in this screenshot:

The following payload fields of the request body of the service are required and can’t be left empty:

  • plant
  • plantName
  • descriptionShort
  • sourceSystemId

Here you have the following options:

  • Create a start parameter for the production process and assign the parameter to the payload field in the right pane of the designer app.
  • Hardcode a value in double quotes, for example, for sourceSystemID enter “Alert in SAP Digital Manufacturing”.
  • Include additional services from the service registry into the production process that perform lookups and pass on looked up values as parameters to the payload field. For example, you can use the service “Reads Plants” to look up the “description” of a plant “1010” and pass it on as a parameter to the payload field “plantName”.

Let’s continue by defining start parameters for the payload fields “plant” and “descriptionShort”:

  1. Select the Start control in the graphical editor, click on “Manage Parameters” in the right pane and add the two parameters “plant” and “descriptionShort” as required parameters of data type “String” and save.
  2. Now, select the rectangular box representing the service in the graphical editor. The right pane will now show the payload fields of its request body. Click on the dropdown icon next to the payload parameters “descriptionShort” and “plant” and select the start parameters you have just created accordingly

You can provide values for start parameters in the following ways:

  • Pass on values from alert properties. This is configured in the “Manage Alert Types” app at design time and will be explained further below. This option is useful for the “Plant” field as most alert types have plant as an alert property.
  • Enter the values manually while managing alerts in the Manage Alert appat runtime when clicking the action button – for example, to enter the “descriptionShort”.

Next, we will provide hard-coded values "Plant Walldorf" and "Alert in SAP Digital Manufacturing" for the required payload parameters “plantName” and “sourceSystemId” respectively. This assumes that you are dealing with one plant only so that you can conveniently hard code it. Note that there is usually no alert property available for it to pass on its value to a start parameter. Simply enter the two values with double quotes in the respective fields in the right pane:

Now, you can click “Save All” and then “Quick Deploy” to test your set up. Click on “Debug” and enter you plant ID and a short description of the quality issue in the respective fields of the pop-up window and click on “Debug”:

This will bring you to the “Monitor Production Processes” screen. On this screen, you can run each individual step in your production process by clicking on the next step button.

The blue circle indicates the next step to execute in the debugger. In the screenshot below, the service has been executed successfully. In the Processing Step Log the responses (or in case of errors, the respective error messages) are displayed. As you can see, a new issue with number 400000000000772 has been created in QIR. The test was successful. You can log on to QIR and search for this issue in there.

Let’s return to the assumption of having just one plant so that the “plantName” field can be conveniently hardcoded. If this assumption is false and you run multiple plants, then you may want to look up the plant name from the plant ID at runtime in the production process. Add the “Reads Plants” service from service registry into the production process as a first step:

  1. In the graphical editor, click on the Edit button to create a new version of the production process design.
  2. On the left pane, click the pencil icon and search for the service “Reads Plants” and expand the “plant APIs” node.
  3. Select the first arrow in the canvas connecting the Start control with the service box and delete the arrow.
  4. Drag and drop the “Reads Plants” service from the left pane into the canvas

  5. Connect the Start control with the new “Reads Plants” box and the box with the “QIR_Issue_Creation” box. While selecting the “Reads Plants” box the right pane shows the payload of the request body of this service. It only has the “plant” field. Click on the dropdown next to it and select the start parameter “plant”.
  6. Now, select the other rectangular box on the canvas to display the payload of the request body of the QIR_Issue_Creation service. Click the dropdown icon next to the payload field “plantName” and select “Service_Read_Plant#httpResponse” line item.

  7. This will open a popup. Select the “description” line and click save:

  8. Now, click “Save All”, then “Quick Deploy” and test with the Debugger as explained above:

     


It looks up the plant description in the “Manage Plants” app and passes on the value to the payload field “plantName”:

You’ll have noticed that many payload fields of the request body of the service QIR_Issue_Creation were left empty. Depending on your scenario and individual process requirements, you can either hard code them in the production process design, have the alert processor enter them manually when clicking on the action button in the Manage Alert apps, or keep them empty and leave it to the quality engineer who picks up the issue in SAP Quality Issue Resolution to populate those fields.

Configure Action Button for Alert Type

Since we have successfully set up the production process that calls the service to create an issue in QIR via API call, we are now ready to bind this production process to an action button for any of our alert types – be it SAP predefined or custom defined:

  1.  Open the “Manage Alert Types” app on the launchpad and choose your alert type – in the example below we have chosen alert type “Quality Alert”:
  2. Enter the details view of the alert type, click Edit, and click on the “Actions” tab. On this tab, click on “Create Action” and select “Production Process”.
  3. Enter a name for the label to appear on the alert detail UI, for example, “Create Issue”, and select the production process “P_QIR_CreateIssue”. In the popup that appears, click on the search icon within the field for “plant” and select the property “DME_Plant” from the alert property set type “DME_Shopfloor”:

    The “descriptionShort” field remains empty. It will be entered at runtime when an alert processor clicks on the action button “Create Issue”. Finally click on Add.

A new action has been created for the selected alert type:

The other action “Notify Quality Engineer” is another type of action. It creates a second action button in the alert detail view with the same label to send an email with predefined text body to predefined recipients (which can both be altered at runtime).

Save the alert type.

This concludes the configuration.

entry.title: profit loss statement fro Poland
entry.content:

i have a customer in China. and they have a sub company in Poland. and according to their description their local p&l report don't list the operation cost and need show the inventory change & material consumption & labor & depreciation and so on.

itemifrslocalvalue

3operation costinventory change

5 labor 
6 depreciation

8   
......

entry.title: Extracting contactperson (table PA0105, subtype KONP) via 0EMPLOYEE_ATTR in RSA3
entry.content:
Hi Gurus, 

 

I'm trying to extract the contactperson of employees via RSA3.

(I have them maintained in table PA0105, subtype KONP)

 

My issue is that for some records it does bring back a value, whereas for others it doesn't.

I can't find any discrepancies between the records other than for some of them the timestamp on PA0000 being slightly different than the one in PA0105, however both being active at a given date - could this really be the cause of missing data?

I'm finding it hard to debug where the tcode RSA3 actually finds the data in S/4 (like does it go directly to the PAxxxx tables) and how it determines if it should bring back a value or not.

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud SM: Introducing the Environment Access Management Screen
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community members!

I’m excited to announce the launch of our brand-new Environment Access Management screen in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM). This new feature fulfills our Cloud users’ need for enhanced security in managing access to environments.

With this, you can now control the visibility to your environments by setting them as either “Public” or “Private”. This ensures that only authorized users have access.

It's essential to note that admin users must possess one of the following roles to utilize this new screen:

  • ENVIRONMENTS_ACCESS_ALL
  • ADMIN_USER_ALL
  • ADMIN_USER_RESTRICTED

Here's an in-depth overview along with the instructions on how to use it:

Navigating to the Screen

To open the Environment Access Management screen, first choose the Menu option.
Next, navigate to Administration, and then choose Environments Access:

The Environment Access Management screen provides a list of all environments available in the system including the following information:

  • Select (checkbox): Mark the environment as needed
  • Environment Description: Displays details of the environment
  • Environment: Defines the identifier of the environment
  • Version: Defines the version of the environment
  • Access: Indicates the access level (“Public” or “Private”) for each environment
  • Authorized Teams: Contains a list of teams who have authorized access to each environment


Note: The screen follows the existing application standards, such as layout, column options, column filters, and so on.
 

Understanding Access Levels

Access levels determine who can view and perform actions with different environments within an application. These levels can either be public or private.

1. Public: By default, environments are set to “Public”. When an environment is “Public”, all users of the application have access to it.
2. Private: Access to environments is restricted to users who are granted permissions fulfilling one of the following conditions:

  • As an administrator, having any of the following roles:
    -  ENVIRONMENTS_ACCESS_ALL
    -  ADMIN_USER_ALL
    - ADMIN_USER_RESTRICTED
  • Being member of a team that has been granted access to particular environment

Access restrictions are enforced across all screens that display an environment list, such as Environments, Process Scheduler (used for job creation), Content Management (used for exporting an environment), and others.

Setting Access Level to Environment

Managing access to various environments is crucial for maintaining security and control.
Here’s a guide on how to set access levels to ensure that only authorized users can view and access designated environments.

1. Select the environment(s) for which you want to set the access level by marking the corresponding checkbox. Note that environments are set to “Public” by default.

2. Choose the Edit access for environment(s) icon in the header section to open the dialog.
The dialog includes the following details:

  • Title: Edit access for environment(s)
  • Description: Adjusting access affects all selected environments
  • Placeholder list of the environments selected by the user
  • Access: Radio buttons allow you to select either “Public” or “Private” option
    When a user selects “Private”, the Authorized Teams multiselect component is shown
  • Buttons: CONFIRM and CANCEL

Dialog: Access (Public)

Dialog: Access (Private)

3. Set Access to “Private” and fill in the Authorized Teams by selecting from the dropdown list.

4. Choose CONFIRM to apply the setting.
The access level for "ABC Environment" has been changed from “Public” to “Private”.
The "ABC-Team" is now assigned to the Authorized Teams field.

Access to Environment when Configured as Private

Let’s explore the outcomes of setting environments to “Private” for the following scenarios:

Scenario 1: Privileged Role Access
Condition:

  • User Role: Users must have one of the following roles:
    -  ENVIRONMENTS_ACCESS_ALL
    -  ADMIN_USER_ALL
    - ADMIN_USER_RESTRICTED
  • Access: Environment is set to “Private”
  • Authorized Teams: This field is empty/blank or the user is not a member of any team assigned to this field

Expected Result: The user will be able to view and access all environments (both public and private)

Summary: In this scenario, any of the roles ENVIRONMENTS_ACCESS_ALL, ADMIN_USER_ALL, OR ADMIN_USER_RESTRICTED grants the user permissions that override access controls based on access level and authorized teams’ membership. As a result, the user will be able to view all environments within the system.

Sample Screens:
Team Management Screen
The user who will be updating the Environment Access Management screen by changing the Access Level and assigning the Authorized Teams must not be member of the ”AAA Team”

Environment Access Management
For the ”ABC Environment”, access is set to “Private”. The “AAA Team” is assigned as authorized team.
For the “AAA Test Env”, access is also set “Private”, but no teams are authorized.
All other environments are set to “Public”.

As you can see, the user has access to all environments, including "ABC Environment" and "AAA Test Env," as shown on the following screens:

Environments

Process Scheduler when adding a Job


Content Management when Exporting an Environment



Scenario 2: Authorized Team Membership Granting Access
Condition:

  • User Role: Users must not possess any of the following roles:
    -  ENVIRONMENTS_ACCESS_ALL
    -  ADMIN_USER_ALL
    - ADMIN_USER_RESTRICTED
  • Access: Environment is set to “Private”
  • Authorized Teams: User is a member of at least one of the teams assigned for this field to access environments

Expected Result: The user can access private environments if he/she belongs to at least one of the authorized teams. Additionally, they will have access to all public environments.

Summary: This scenario demonstrates how access to private environments is controlled based on team membership authorization.

Sample Screens:
Team Management Screen
The user who will be updating the Environment Access Management screen by changing the Access Level and assigning the Authorized Teams must meet the following criteria:
Must be a member of the “ABC-Team”

Must not be a member of the “AAA Team”

Environment Access Management
For the ”ABC Environment”, access is set to “Private”. The ”AAA Team” and ”ABC-Team” are assigned as authorized teams.
For the ”AAA Test Env”, access is also set to “Private”, but no teams are authorized.
For the ”AAA Environment”, access is also set to “Private”, and the ”AAA Team” is assigned as authorized team.
All other environments are set to “Public”.

How can you identify which private environments the user can access based on the given criteria?
For private environments, users can view the ”ABC Environment” only because he/she is a member to at least one of the authorized teams. However, the “AAA Test Env” and ”AAA Environment” will not be visible to the user. Furthermore, the user will have access to all public environments.

Environments



Process Scheduler when adding a Job


Content Management when Exporting an Environment




That’s a wrap for this blog post! I hope you find this overview of the new Environment Access Management screen beneficial.

Stay tuned for the latest updates and insights on the next one. Thank you!

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud: What’s New as of 2024-07-02?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community members!   

Hi Community members, I am thrilled to bring you the latest updates related to SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud (SAP PaPM Cloud) for the month of June as my first blog post. 

Now, let's jump straight into the newest features and improvements in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud (SAP PaPM Cloud).    

Note: This blog post contains images. In case the images are too small, kindly click on the image to view with a better resolution.

History Monitor 

The new enhancement in the History Monitor is related to log data when users delete logs from History Monitor. In SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud (SAP PaPM Cloud) Standard Model, the Erase Data functionality is enabled for users with SYSTEM_USER_ALL and MODELING_HISTORY. Previously, this allowed users to delete History snapshot without any logs that provide who, when and what is deleted in the application in any monitor screen.  

 With the enhancement, when the user with previously mentions rules delete/erase History logs from the History Monitor, the action is now logged in the Application Monitor under the Run Type H_DELETE specifies who deleted the records and when the deletion take place. 

Writer

The Process Management screen has a feature where the users have an option to lock and unlock data slices in the underlying Model Tables of an Editable Query function used by the Input/Output Activity in the Process Management screen. 

This data locking is not being considered by the Writer function when the Model Table is used as its Output function. With the new enhancement, running the Writer function will be cancelled if there are some data slices locked in the Result Model Table.  

See image below for example logs:  

If you would like to learn more about this data slice lock feature, you can refer to this blog post: What's new as of 2022-12-19  

Environment Access Management Screen 

SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud (SAP PaPM Cloud) is introducing a new application called Environment Access Management screen, this allows users to manage Environments visibility by designating them as Public or Private, ensuring that only authorized personnel can view and access the Environments. This will provide more security for the data. 

More details about the Environment Access Management Screen can be found in the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud (SAP PaPM Cloud) blogpost: Introducing the New Environment Access Management Screen.

I hope that you have found this blogpost interesting and insightful to the new features in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud (SAP PaPM Cloud) Standard Model. 

To keep you up to date with our earlier blog posts on the newest developments and features in SAP PaPM Cloud SM, you can check out this tag, which contains a complete archive of related content: PaPMCloudWhatsNew 

Thank you and stay tuned for the future What's New series!

entry.title: Why QR IBAN is automatically prefilled in the invoice
entry.content:
If the Supplier has only a QR IBAN defined in the master data (IBAN with interval 30000-31999 on positions 5-9), then the QR IBAN is automatically prefilled to the incoming invoice during the posting. The accountant must provide only a QR Reference (a unique 27chars reference that is required for a QR invoice).

This ensures that the incoming invoice is posted correctly and the payment will not be rejected later by the payment program due to missing QR data.

If the Supplier has other regular IBANs in the master data in addition to the QR IBAN, the QR IBAN is not filled in automatically and all other invoice types can be posted.

entry.title: Navigating the speed bumps across the supply chain
entry.content:
SAP and Samsung transform the mobile frontline worker experience for supply chains

At SAP Sapphire Orlando in June 2024 we could hear from our senior executives that 97% of the supply chains got disrupted in the last 2 years. They emphasized the importance of a flexible supply chain to meet personalized customer demands as well as the need of agility to sense, orchestrate and respond to short-term disruption events intelligently.

At SAP Digital Supply Chain Management, we believe in the power of partnerships. Our commitment to delivering innovative experiences to empower and transform our customers mobile frontline workers has led us to continually seek cutting-edge mobile partnerships.

An end-to-end approach, adoptable and tailored to various mobile workforce needs can help you to take on some of the most common challenges.

  1. Taskforce shortage: Digitalization can help work more efficiently, but also ease the administrative burden. 
  1. Employee experience: AI copilot Joule is there to ask a question or frame an issue to receive intelligent answers. More and more will be possible on-device.
  1. Rising costs: Controlling your costs and optimizing processes becoming crucial with pressure on margins.

So, what does a technology solution that cost-effectively delivers results, while simultaneously enhancing the mobile frontline worker experience and enabling them to efficiently do their jobs, look like? It surely starts with modernizing the mobile work environment of frontline workers end to end. Mobile-enabled processes and extensible form factors such as phone, tablet and wearable devices integrated with SAP enterprise systems enable easy and timely execution on the go. Our strategic partnership with Samsung enables us to deliver on the following modernization and enhancement goals with robust hardware, intelligent applications and delightful user experiences.

  1. Improve Operational Resilience: Providing intelligence and automation for all field tasks on the spot of your digital supply chain mobile workforce. Boost operational efficiency with SAP solutions integrated with Samsung Galaxy rugged devices and Knox solutions.
  1. Transform Work Experience: Empowering frontline workers to work whenever and wherever work takes them. Providing a persona-centric native mobile experience at your technician’s fingertips with a seamless data capture experience with Samsung Knox Suite*.
  1. Secure and Simplify: Providing SAP Service and Asset Manager natively integrated with Galaxy XCover and Tab Active series along with Knox solutions for ease of deployment. All data captured by joint offering are securely protected and managed from Samsung devices to and seamlessly integrated into SAP Digital Supply Chain Management.

The Future of Supply Chain podcast 

Before we continue I would like to alert you to a first joint podcast with Samsung’s Phil Landers in the “The Future of Supply Chain” series “Episode 66: Mobilizing the World of Asset and Service Management” right here. Together with our supply chain experts Phil provides an immensive insight into how Samsung helps to drive the future of supply chain for frontline workers and how customers benefit with our partnership. Go check it out!

SAP Service and Asset Manager and Samsung Knox Capture 

Out of the benefits intended to be delivered later this year I would like to focus on the integration of our mobile flagship application SAP Service and Asset Manager and Samsung Knox Capture for the use case of data capture using barcodes. With our persona-centric approach we have identified valuable integration points along the day-in-the-life of a maintenance technician and inventory clerk.

Screenshot: SAP Service and Asset Manager on Samsung Galaxy Tab Active5
*Image simulated for illustrative purposes. Actual UI may be different.

The Samsung Knox Capture integration with SAP Service and Asset Manager will help enable the user to  scan barcodes and automate filling of input fields during maintenance and inventory activities. The programmable Hardware Key on Samsung Galaxy rugged devices will also enable 1 click trigger of scanning functionality, allowing users to optimize their daily activity by enabling them to scan single or multi barcodes simultaneously based on their use case needs with the simple press of a hardware key.

Screenshot: SAP Service and Asset Manager on Samsung Galaxy XCover7
*Image simulated for illustrative purposes. Actual UI may be different

SAP Build Code on SAP Business Technology Platform and Samsung Galaxy rugged devices with Samsung Knox Capture

All these capabilities are powered by SAP Build Code on SAP BTP.  The experience extends beyond SAP's applications. Enterprise developers can leverage SAP Build Code and Samsung Knox solutions to create state-of-the-art experiences optimized for Samsung devices. By using the SDKs provided by SAP and Samsung, developers can accelerate application development and deliver cutting-edge solutions that transform enterprise workflows. Furthermore, administrators can easily manage features and monitor usage through dedicated dashboards provided by SAP and Samsung, ensuring efficient and secure operations.

In summary, this partnership helps you to successfully transform and run all your field tasks in a resilient and future-proof way.

Keep in touch

Visit the SAP Mobile Experience Community Page and click “follow” to get the latest development and innovation of our solutions. We look forward to hearing about your experience with setting up the solution in your landscape; please do share your thoughts and comments below. Enter here for additional questions regarding SAP Mobile Experience Applications.

Want to be notified? Check your profile settings to ensure you have your settings activated.

DSC call to action? Learn more about SAP Service and Asset Manager on www.sap.com/am.

See you again soon for the next update!📲

*Please note that Samsung Knox Suite license is required to use Knox Capture. See details on SamsungKnox.com

entry.title: Analyzing User Behavior to Enhance SAP Fiori and SAP UI5 Apps
entry.content:
The success of enterprise applications greatly depends on their effectiveness. When apps load slowly, have complicated interfaces, or malfunction, it leads to frustration and decreased productivity among users. To avoid these issues, product owners must prioritize both user experience and productivity from the beginning of development and continuously refine their applications for usability and efficiency. During the development of SAP Fiori apps, I have also encountered these challenges and gained valuable experience in addressing them.

Understanding user satisfaction and productivity through surveys and objective data on user behavior is essential for improving business applications. Monitoring and analyzing how users interact with SAP Fiori apps can provide valuable insights. By examining usage patterns, identifying performance bottlenecks, and detecting errors, administrators and developers can make informed decisions to enhance both the overall user experience and the productivity of the end-users.

Analyzing User Behavior in SAP Fiori and SAP UI5 Apps

To effectively analyze user behavior in SAP Fiori applications, we have developed a comprehensive solution that offers various analysis functions. This solution collects, analyzes, and visualizes usage data, helping identify anomalies and relevant patterns. Here are some key aspects of our user behavior analysis:

 

  1. Service Usage: Understanding which data services of SAP Fiori apps are used, how often, and when. This information can be filtered and sorted by time and service, providing insights into user preferences and potential performance issues. For example, if a particular service is accessed frequently, it could indicate its high relevance or a usability problem if it’s a function that should typically be used less often.
  2. Error Monitoring: Documenting and displaying all errors that occur. Errors can be filtered and sorted, helping developers pinpoint and address the root causes of issues. Frequent errors in certain areas can highlight where code improvements or user interface adjustments are needed.
  3. Performance Analysis: Analyzing response times for service calls to identify services causing poor performance. Monitoring the traffic of service calls shows how much data is transferred during calls, allowing for optimization of data-heavy processes. For instance, if certain calls take longer due to large data transfers, developers can look into data compression or more efficient data handling techniques.
  4. Browser and Theme Usage: Listing the browsers and Fiori themes used by users to understand preferences and draw conclusions about browser-based hurdles or theme-specific usability issues. This can help in optimizing the application for the most commonly used browsers and themes, ensuring a consistent and smooth user experience.
  5. Language Preferences: Tracking the languages used by users can provide insights into translation accuracy and help plan future releases. If users often switch from their native language interface to another language, it could indicate translation issues that need to be addressed to improve user satisfaction.
  6. Time Spent in Applications: Recording how long users spend in each SAP Fiori app, which can be filtered and sorted by app and time, helps identify high-engagement areas or potential usability problems. If users spend too much time on certain tasks, it might indicate that those tasks are too complex or not intuitive enough.

 

Improving User Experience with Data-Driven Insights

The insights gained from analyzing user behavior can be used to improve the stability, performance, and usability of SAP Fiori apps. For instance, if users frequently switch from their native language frontend to the English one, it might indicate significant translation errors. Similarly, if a particular service is used very frequently, it could suggest high relevance or a usability issue if it’s supposed to be a minor function.

By continuously monitoring and analyzing these data points, developers and administrators can proactively address issues, enhance application stability, and improve both the overall user experience and productivity. Regularly reviewing this data ensures that the applications remain user-centric and meet the evolving needs of their users.

Feedback and Ideas?

As we continue to develop our solution to monitor and analyze user behavior in SAP Fiori apps, we are eager to gather feedback and insights from the community. What criteria do you find most useful for understanding user behavior in SAP Fiori apps? Are there specific features or functionalities that you believe would enhance user experience and productivity analysis?

Your input and experiences are important to us as we strive to build a comprehensive tool that meets the needs of administrators, key users, and developers alike. Please share your thoughts and suggestions.

entry.title: A Comprehensive Guide to Using OLE Objects in SAP ABAP
entry.content:
In this blog, we’ll explore how to use OLE objects in SAP ABAP to automate tasks in Microsoft Word. We will cover the basics, including how to create an OLE object, manipulate data, and handle errors.

Introduction to OLE in SAP ABAP

OLE Automation in ABAP enables you to control Microsoft Office applications. You can create documents, fill in data, format cells, and even run macros—all from within your ABAP programs. This is particularly useful for generating reports, exporting data, and performing complex calculations.

Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure that:

You have access to a Microsoft Office installation on the machine running the SAP GUI.

You have basic knowledge of ABAP programming.

Creating an OLE Object

To start using OLE in ABAP, you first need to create an OLE object. The CREATE OBJECT statement in ABAP is used for this purpose.

This code will extract the data from database table ‘mara’ and populate into the word file using the concept of OLE .

 

 

REPORT ZTEST_BLOGYS.
PARAMETERS: p_file  TYPE localfile DEFAULT 'C:\Users\Yeshica.Sharma\Downloads\p0.docx'.   “we are declaring file pathwhere we  need to store word file. 
INCLUDE ole2incl.

 

We are declaring data and creating ole object

 

DATA:        “we are declaring data for ole object
  ***      OLE Object
  gs_word           TYPE    ole2_object, "Word Object
  gs_documents      TYPE ole2_object, "List of documents
  gs_document       TYPE ole2_object, "Current / Active document
  gs_selection      TYPE ole2_object, "Current Cursor Selection
  gs_actdoc         TYPE ole2_object , "Active document
  gs_font           TYPE ole2_object , "Font
  gs_parformat      TYPE ole2_object , "Paragraph format
  gs_tables         TYPE ole2_object , "Tables
  gs_table          TYPE ole2_object,
  gs_range          TYPE ole2_object,
  gs_cell           TYPE ole2_object,
  gs_border         TYPE ole2_object,
  gs_interior       TYPE ole2_object,
  gs_application    TYPE ole2_object, "Application
  gv_pos(5)         TYPE n , "Position information for table
  wf_lines          TYPE i,
  wf_counter        TYPE i,
  lv_date           TYPE char15,
  lv_data           TYPE string,
  lv_copyrightdata  TYPE string.
  "Create word object
   CREATE OBJECT gs_word 'WORD.APPLICATION'.
* Setting object's visibility property
  SET PROPERTY OF gs_word 'Visible' = 1.
*  Opening a new document
  CALL METHOD OF gs_word 'Documents' = gs_documents.
  CALL METHOD OF gs_documents 'Add' = gs_document.
*  Activating the sheet
  CALL METHOD OF gs_document 'Activate'.
* Getting active document handle
  GET PROPERTY OF gs_word 'ActiveDocument' = gs_actdoc.
*  Getting applications handle
  GET PROPERTY OF gs_actdoc 'Application' = gs_application.
* Getting handle for the selection which is here the character at the cursor position
  GET PROPERTY OF gs_application 'Selection' = gs_selection.
  GET PROPERTY OF gs_selection 'Font' = gs_font.
  GET PROPERTY OF gs_selection 'ParagraphFormat' = gs_parformat.

 

We are declaring data and getting a data from Database table Mara

 

Types : Begin of ty_mara,
        Matnr type mara-matnr,
        ersda type mara-ersda,
        ernam type mara-ernam,
        end of ty_mara.
data : it_mara type table of mara,
       wa_mara type mara.
select matnr ersda ernam from mara into corresponding fields of table it_mara UP TO 5 ROWS.

 

We are creating table and populating data into a word file

 

CALL METHOD OF gs_selection 'TypeText'
    EXPORTING
      #1 = 'DATA OF MARA TABLE'.
      GET PROPERTY OF gs_actdoc 'Tables' = gs_tables.
      GET PROPERTY OF gs_selection 'Range' = gs_range.
      DESCRIBE TABLE it_mara LINES wf_lines.
      wf_lines = wf_lines + 1.
      IF sy-subrc NE 0. 
      ENDIF.
      CALL METHOD OF gs_tables 'Add' = gs_table
              EXPORTING #1 = gs_range
                        #2 = wf_lines
                        #3 = '3'. "Columns
      GET PROPERTY OF gs_table 'Borders' = gs_border.
      SET PROPERTY OF gs_border 'Enable' = '1'.
      IF sy-subrc NE 0. 
      ENDIF.
      CALL METHOD OF gs_table 'Cell' = gs_cell
      EXPORTING #1 = '1'
      #2 = '1'.
*  *setting the bg color
      CALL METHOD OF gs_cell 'Shading' = gs_interior.
      SET PROPERTY OF gs_interior 'BackgroundPatternColor' = -570376193.
      GET PROPERTY OF gs_cell 'Range' = gs_range.
      SET PROPERTY OF gs_range 'Text' = 'matnr'.
      CALL METHOD OF gs_range 'Font' = gs_font.
      SET PROPERTY OF gs_font 'Bold' = '1'. " Make the text bold
      CALL METHOD OF gs_range 'SetFont' = gs_font.
      IF sy-subrc NE 0. ENDIF.
************************* Col 2
      CALL METHOD OF gs_table 'Cell' = gs_cell
        EXPORTING #1 = '1'
        #2 = '2'.
*  *setting the bg color
      CALL METHOD OF gs_cell 'Shading' = gs_interior.
      SET PROPERTY OF gs_interior 'BackgroundPatternColor' = -570376193.
      GET PROPERTY OF gs_cell 'Range' = gs_range.
      SET PROPERTY OF gs_range 'Text' = 'ersda'.
      CALL METHOD OF gs_range 'Font' = gs_font.
      SET PROPERTY OF gs_font 'Bold' = '1'. " Make the text bold
      CALL METHOD OF gs_range 'SetFont' = gs_font.
      IF sy-subrc NE 0. ENDIF.
**********************  Col 3
      CALL METHOD OF gs_table 'Cell' = gs_cell
        EXPORTING #1 = '1'
        #2 = '3'.
*  *setting the bg color
      CALL METHOD OF gs_cell 'Shading' = gs_interior.
      SET PROPERTY OF gs_interior 'BackgroundPatternColor' = -570376193.
      GET PROPERTY OF gs_cell 'Range' = gs_range.
      SET PROPERTY OF gs_range 'Text' = 'ernam'.
      CALL METHOD OF gs_range 'Font' = gs_font.
      SET PROPERTY OF gs_font 'Bold' = '1'. " Make the text bold
      CALL METHOD OF gs_range 'SetFont' = gs_font.
      IF sy-subrc NE 0. 
      ENDIF.
      wf_counter = 1.
      LOOP AT it_mara INTO wa_mara.
      wf_counter = wf_counter + 1.
       CALL METHOD OF gs_table 'Cell' = gs_cell
         EXPORTING #1 = wf_counter
         #2 = '1'.
        GET PROPERTY OF gs_cell 'Range' = gs_range.
        SET PROPERTY OF gs_range 'Text' = wa_mara-matnr.
        IF sy-subrc NE 0.
        ENDIF.
       CALL METHOD OF gs_table 'Cell' = gs_cell
          EXPORTING #1 = wf_counter
          #2 = '2'.
        GET PROPERTY OF gs_cell 'Range' = gs_range.
        SET PROPERTY OF gs_range 'Text' = wa_mara-ersda.
        IF sy-subrc NE 0.
        ENDIF.
       CALL METHOD OF gs_table 'Cell' = gs_cell
          EXPORTING #1 = wf_counter
          #2 = '3'.
        GET PROPERTY OF gs_cell 'Range' = gs_range.
        SET PROPERTY OF gs_range 'Text' = wa_mara-ernam.
        IF sy-subrc NE 0.
        ENDIF.
      ENDLOOP.
*--Advancing the cursor to the end of the table
      GET PROPERTY OF gs_table 'Range' = gs_range .
      GET PROPERTY OF gs_range 'End' = gv_pos .
      SET PROPERTY OF gs_range 'Start' = gv_pos .
      CALL METHOD OF gs_range 'Select' .
      DO 2 TIMES.
        CALL METHOD OF gs_selection 'TypeParagraph' .
      ENDDO.
      IF sy-subrc NE 0.
      ENDIF.

 

Output is like below -

Conclusion
Integrating OLE objects in SAP ABAP provides a seamless way to leverage the powerful functionalities of Microsoft Office applications directly within your SAP environment. By automating tasks in Excel, Word, or PowerPoint, you can enhance productivity, streamline workflows, and create dynamic reports. Understanding how to create and manipulate OLE objects in ABAP opens up a myriad of possibilities for SAP developers, allowing for the creation of sophisticated solutions that integrate the best of SAP and Microsoft Office capabilities. With the steps and examples provided, you are now equipped to start incorporating OLE automation into your ABAP programs, making your SAP applications more versatile and efficient.


Hope this may helpful.

entry.title: Future Dated Hires Provisioning from SF EC/Onboarding to Azure AD / Onprem AD
entry.content:
Hi Everyone, 

We have recently implemented the the standard and flexible  solution to replicate worker/user/employee data from SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central to  Azure AD/Onprem AD  and it was an amazing successful implementation . The standard solution is  in collaboration of  SAP SuccessFactros and Microsoft  without any middleware with minimal configuration in SF EC and in MS ENTRA. This is an excellent and seamless integration to replicate employee data to Azure AD/ Onprem AD . 

Initially I was planning  of providing end to end solution steps from my experience but  I found one of our colleagues already provided the same and you can refer below blog for complete solution. 

https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blogs-by-members/bidirectional-integration-bet...

Please find below architectural overview  of the entire solution and is self explanatory. 

 

In my blog, I would like to talk about the challenging issues that we have faced to replicate future dated hires though they are inactive in SAP SuccessFactros Employee Central. 

As per current solution,  if the employee is hired directly in SF EC without onboarding with future dated hire date then it will be replicated/provisioned to Azure AD/ AD seamlessly wihtout any issues. 

However, if hiring will be completed in Onboarding module  and then employees are flowing into SF EC after all approvals there is an issue. 

If Employee hired in Onboarding with future dated and user account will be inactive until the hire date reaches in both SF EC and Onboarding as well. 

Hence, when Azure AD/ AD interface tries to pull these future dated hires from SF EC then these users will  be skipped. The normal  SAP SF EC API user which is configured in Azure AD /AD cannot read these users due to RBP issue. There is a need to provide extra RBP  to read the future dated hires which are inactive in SF EC and  SF EC is treated them as All Onboarding Externals. 

Hence, to ensure Azure AD/AD  to extract these future dated hires directly  from SF EC/Onboarding  specific  RBP needs to be setup  to the API user that has been created in SF EC by giving target population as All(External Onboarding User). 

Hope this helps if anyone facing the same issue and it takes time to find and rectify because everything looks fine  but issue cannot resolved. 

 

I request you to go through and follow many latest integration topics as below for better knowledge and understanding.

 

https://community.sap.com/topics/ariba-cloud-integration-gateway

You can also browse below to post a question or answer a question.

https://answers.sap.com/tags/67837800100800006801

https://blogs.sap.com/tags/67837800100800006801/

 

Thank you Very Much,

Prakash J

entry.title: Data Federation in Datasphere
entry.content:
Data Federation in SAP Datasphere

Data federation is a powerful data management strategy that enables seamless integration and efficient utilization of disparate data sources. In this approach, we don’t consolidate information or eliminate other sources; instead, we leave the data where it resides and use virtualization techniques to provide a unified view

                                

  1. How It Works:
    • Data federation doesn’t move data around; it keeps it in its original location.
    • Virtualization magic happens: We create a unified view of data from various systems, making it appear as if it’s all in one place.
    • Federated queries allow easy access to information across different platforms.
  2. Why It Matters:
    • Data Explosion: With data multiplying like rabbits, we need efficient ways to manage it.
    • Better Analytics: Federated systems enhance data integration and analytics.
    • Customer Interactions: Businesses can provide relevant info by connecting diverse sources without redundant copies.
  3. Not Data Consolidation:
    • Data federation ≠ data consolidation.
    • Federated architecture respects autonomy while integrating data sources.

Benefits

SAP Datasphere

SAP Datasphere, a comprehensive data service built on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) that enables every data professional to deliver seamless and scalable access to mission-critical business data.

With the unified experience of SAP Datasphere for data integration, data cataloging, semantic modeling, data warehousing, data federation, and data virtualization, data professionals can now easily distribute mission-critical business data — with business context and logic preserved — across their organization’s data landscape

Data Federation in Datasphere - Virtually access and store data from anywhere

       Designed to enable seamless integration and real-time access to data across various sources

  • It employs a virtual database approach, allowing users to create virtual tables that aggregate data from different sources without duplicating it.
  • SAP Datasphere’s data federation architecture is supported by tools like the SAP HANA smart data integration data provisioning agent and adapters like the MSSQL Log Reader Adapter
  • These tools help in federating data from platforms such as Azure Synapse into SAP Datasphere, thus leveraging its analytical capabilities through SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Data Federation avoids extracting the data from SAP S/4HANA
  • Remote Table Federation: First, you can federate data, where you leave the data in the source system and access it remotely in real-time
  • Model Transfer approach: BW/4HANA rich metadata is imported from BW/4HANA to Datasphere, while the data is being virtually accessed
  • The architecture also includes the Federated-ML or FedML library, which addresses challenges of data migration and replication. It intelligently sources data in real-time from data storages for building, training, and deploying machine learning models on hyperscalers without the need to replicate or migrate data.                                 

Conclusion

Overall, SAP Datasphere’s data federation architecture harmonizes mission-critical data throughout the organization, providing a unified service for data integration, cataloging, semantic modeling, and data warehousing, thereby enabling a business data fabric architecture.

entry.title: Best Practices while Migrating Commisions from Oracle to SAP from Analytics Perspective
entry.content:
In this blog we will discuss on the best practices we need to follow while migrating SAP Commissions from Oracle to SAP HANA

Estimation

  • Before giving estimation of completion of project, one should consider manual work needed to convert Oracle Objects (Procedures, Packages, Tables) to HANA. Although SAP has provided Tool to convert Oracle SQL code to HANA, but since this tool is designed by keeping generic solution in consideration, we need manual Intervention and SQL Knowledge Convert it Successfully to HANA.
  • We need to consider No of Reports, Types of report (Crystal/Webi) and complexity of each report at the time of estimation.
  • Since many times, Custom Models need to be created using CSN file, we need to keep that also in consideration.
  • Type of Security which was applied in SAP Commissions is entirely different from Oracle security, so need to setup that as well.
  • Since Crystal, WEBI reporting solutions are entirely different from SAC(Embedded)Solutions, so some of the functionalities of Crystal and WEBI does not match with SAC , so finalising design with client as per SAC should be kept in consideration.

Converting Oracle Objects to HANA

Once you have converted Oracle object to HANA using Oracle to HANA migration Tool, you need to manually adjust below query changes.

  • Prefix Schema name (EXT, TCMP etc) before every Table/Procedure name.
  • Data Type Number (Oracle) should be changed to Integer (HANA).
  • Exception Handling in Oracle is done at end of procedure but in HANA it is done at beginning after Begin Keyword.
  • Comment all unnecessary statements starting with EXECUTE IMMEDIATE.
  • Comment all unnecessary CREATE STATISTICS statements.
  • Comment All Partitioning statements if it is not needed as HANA database is strong enough to handle slow processing automatically.
  • Change keyword localtimestamp(6) (Oracle) to CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(HANA).
  • Change TO_CHAR() to TO_NVARCHAR().
  • Change TO_NUMBER() to TO_INTEGER().
  • Instead of Hardcoding tenant id, store the tenant id system in variable and use it in procedure.

select tenantid into v_tenantid from TCMP.CS_TENANT;

  • Try to Push down all the logics written at reporting tool level to procedure level, so that report processing will be fast.
  • Run the Procedure manually and ensure that data in oracle tables is matching with HANA tables.

Creating views on top of HANA Tables

Custom data models in SAP Commissions are built by creating SQL views in Web IDE.

SAP commission database has two default schemas EXT and TCMP. The custom data models that need to be exposed to embedded analytics in SAC should be in EXT schema.

 

  • Ensure that Views have minimum logics/Calculations. All Calculations should be handled at Procedure level for better performance.
  • Ensure to Apply Structured Privileges to views, so that Employee /Manager can see correct data as per their role.
  • For better Visibility and maintenance, try to Keep column sequence of View similar to sequence of base table.

Creating CSN file using views

Custom data model is exposed to Embedded Analytics SAC by creating a CSN file. The CSN file is a JSON format that defines the metadata necessary for the physical tables and views. These tables and views are exposed in the Embedded Analytics for SAP Commissions UI.

 

  • Context of the CSN file must be set to EXT.
  • CSN file must have at least one measure.
  • Rather can creating multiple CSN file, try to add multiple models in single file for better maintenance.
  • @Common.Label annotation must be before @analytics query annotation.
  • For better Visibility and maintenance, try to Keep column sequence of CSN file similar to sequence of base view.
  • Before replacing the old CSN file with new one, download the copy of Old File in local so that, if anything went wrong, changes can be easily reverted.

 

Creating Story in Embedded Analytics 

  • Since Suppression is not supported in SAC, we need to use Widget level filter to filter data as per user.
  • Use Multiple Table in same page and filter each table (Widget) with specific condition (Similar to grouping in Oracle) to implement Suppression Logic.
  • Keep Design crisp and clear.
  • Try to Avoid doing calculation at story level.
  • To Export the data in PDF, try to use Batch Export functionality in SAC.

File --> Export--> Batch Export by Story Filter (Uncheck Insert Appendix) --> Select a filter and click on Export.

  • Book the story so set it as Default and Global, so that when users opens it , it will always open in default state.
  • Try to set Page width , size, so that when report is exported to PDF or Printed, it will have proper visibility.
  • Try to restrict data using Page filters.

For other SAC Best Practises, you can refer to below Blog

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/best-practices-for-performance/ba-p/13518741

Conclusion: You can use the standard models provided out of the box to create custom stories/dashboards as per your needs.

More information about embedded analytics for commissions can be found here . Please feel free to provide any feedback you have in the comments section below and ask your questions about the topic in sap community using this link.

SAP SuccessFactors Incentive Management   SAP Analytics Cloud, connectivity   #SAP Analytics   SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA database

entry.title: Re: Profit Center Valuation with Universal Parallel Accounting in SAP S/4HANA 2023
entry.content:
Thanks Janet - it was a great read, and glad to see Profit Center Valuation for UPA introduced in OP2023, especially with the capability to eliminate COGS and Revenue in Group Valuation.

From what I read on Gerhard Welker's blog on Advanced Intercompany Sales, it feels like this solution is catered towards inventory movements.T

SAP has been introducing a number intercompany billing innovations (e.g. billing document request, new RRICB). Is there any plan for all these solutions to automatically post the elimination journals in ledger 4G?

entry.title: Re: Settlement calculation on the basis of Business volume customer for Single Payee contract
entry.content:
Hi Pankaj,

You have to transfer the related business volume value to the settlement document. 
One of the solution in your case would be to use split criteria configuration.
For customer you can use the field CUSTOMER or your own custom field. 

Also, I see that this field is used for pricing, hence you will need to make sure the value is transferred to the corresponding pricing field in the settlement document for pricing determination. 
For that one of the solution would be to use Transfer Manager. 

Last thought about your post, as you're posting accrual with billing document, you need to make sure the accrual will be reversed correctly at the time of Settlement and not posting an extra line just for accrual reversal. 

If any of the above is new for you, and the configuration/development is needed urgently, I would recommend you and your organization to contact a competent resource with experience in SAP CCM. 

Regards, 
Gauthier
